questions: Locate the 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank (University of Surrey, 2001), which is structured into eight tutorials concluding with 'UNIX variables'. Identify the specific Creative Commons license version applied to this work. According to the official license deed for that specific version, what are the two primary 'Freedoms' granted to the user, what are the three distinct 'Terms' required to exercise those freedoms, and what notice is currently displayed regarding the 4.0 version of the license?

rounds.question: Locate the 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank (University of Surrey, 2001), which is structured into eight tutorials concluding with 'UNIX variables'. Identify the specific Creative Commons license version applied to this work. According to the official license deed for that specific version, what are the two primary 'Freedoms' granted to the user, what are the three distinct 'Terms' required to exercise those freedoms, and what notice is currently displayed regarding the 4.0 version of the license?
checklist_width: Freedom 1: Share (copy and redistribute in any medium or format)
Freedom 2: Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material)
Term 1: Attribution (give credit, provide link, indicate changes)
Term 3: ShareAlike (distribute contributions under the same license)
Notice regarding 4.0: The 4.0 versions are described as more user-friendly and internationally robust; use of 4.0 is recommended.
checklist_depth: Logic Validation: The agent must locate Stonebank's tutorial to find the specific license link, then access the license deed to retrieve the text details.
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by describing the tutorial's structure and author (M. Stonebank) without naming the specific license version, requiring the agent to locate the primary source to find the license link. It utilizes Wide Logic by requiring the agent to parse the license deed to extract specific categories of information (Freedoms, Terms, and Notices) that are distinct from the tutorial content itself.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' by M. Stonebank, hosted by the University of Surrey, is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK) [1]. According to the official license deed for this version, the two primary freedoms granted to users are the right to share (copy and redistribute the material) and the right to adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material) for non-commercial purposes [2]. To exercise these freedoms, three key terms must be followed: (1) Attribution — users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made; (2) Non-Commercial — the material may not be used for commercial purposes; and (3) ShareAlike — if the material is remixed or transformed, the resulting work must be distributed under the same license terms [2]. The license deed also includes a notice stating that Creative Commons is not recommending or endorsing the work; it merely provides the legal framework for sharing [2]. Regarding version 4.0, the current license page notes that the 4.0 version of CC licenses is the most up-to-date and recommends its use for new licensing, as it is more internationally robust and includes improved anti-tivoization and database rights provisions, though the older version remains valid for works licensed under it [3].

## References  
[1] http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Personal/M.Stonebank/  
[2] https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/  
[3] https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
rounds.answer_b:
The "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners" by M. Stonebank, published in October 2001 by the University of Surrey, is available online through the university's Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering website. It consists of an introduction followed by eight tutorials, covering topics from basic file management to advanced features, with the final Tutorial Eight focusing on UNIX variables, including their declaration, use, and environmental applications [1].

This work is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales License (CC BY-SA 2.0 UK) [1]. According to the official license deed for CC BY-SA 2.0 UK, the two primary freedoms granted to users are: (1) to copy, distribute, display, and perform the work; and (2) to make derivative works, including for commercial purposes, provided the terms are followed [2].

The three distinct terms required to exercise these freedoms are: (1) Attribution – users must give appropriate credit to the original author, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made; (2) ShareAlike – if the work is remixed, transformed, or built upon, the resulting derivative must be distributed under the same or equivalent license; and (3) Notice – for any reuse or distribution, users must make clear to others the license terms of the work, such as by including a copy of the license or a link to it [2].

The deed currently displays a notice stating: "We have updated this license. Consider using the latest version, 4.0. Version 4.0 is more user-friendly, internationally oriented, and supports use in offline contexts, among other improvements" [2]. This encourages migration to the newer version while affirming that the 2.0 license remains valid for existing works.

(Word count: 284)

## References
[1] University of Surrey. (2001). UNIX Tutorial for Beginners. Retrieved from https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/

[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). Attribution-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0/uk/deed.en
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A correctly identified the specific license applied to the work (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK). Agent B failed this critical step, identifying it as CC BY-SA (missing the NonCommercial restriction). Because Agent B identified the wrong license, its subsequent listing of freedoms (allowing commercial use) and terms (missing NonCommercial) was factually incorrect for the specific tutorial requested. Agent A was accurate on all counts, although its formatting was slightly dense.
